notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s a simple way to think about any client or business trip. This is general, educational info, not tax advice.

1. How to think about travel rewards and cash-back
Personal vs tax treatment (U.S.)

Points/miles from business spending (on your personal or business card):

Usually treated as a rebate/discount, not income.
That means:
You generally don’t report the value of points/miles as taxable income.
Your deductible business expense is what you actually paid in cash, after discounts or statement credits.

Examples:

You buy a $400 flight for a client trip:
Pay $300 cash + use 10,000 points worth $100.
For tax purposes, your business expense is typically $300, not $400.
You get 2% cash back on a $500 hotel:
You pay $500, get $10 back later.
Economically, your net cost is $490. Many people still record the $500 as the expense and treat the $10 as a reduction later; the key is to be consistent.

Employer/client reimbursements and rewards:

If your employer or client reimburses you for the full cost and you also earn points:
The reimbursement is typically not income if it’s under an “accountable plan” (you submit receipts and return any excess).
The points are usually treated as yours personally and not taxable.
If you get a flat per diem and keep the difference, that can have different tax implications depending on the setup.

Because the rules can be nuanced, many people:

Treat rewards as personal perks.
Focus their records on the actual dollars paid and reimbursed.

Key idea: Track cash, treat rewards as gravy.

2. Tracking reimbursed vs non‑reimbursed expenses

A simple structure makes tax time and client invoicing way easier.

What to capture for each expense

For each item, note:

Date
Vendor (airline, hotel, Uber, restaurant, etc.)
Amount paid (cash)
Payment method (which card)
Business purpose (e.g., “Client A strategy meeting – travel to NYC”)
Reimbursed? (Yes/No)
Reimbursed by whom? (Employer vs specific client)
Portion personal vs business (if mixed)

You can keep this in:

A spreadsheet
Accounting software
A notes app plus your card statements
Reimbursed expenses
Keep a copy of the receipt and proof of reimbursement (pay stub, expense report, client payment).
Mark them clearly as “reimbursed – not deducted” so you don’t accidentally deduct them again on your taxes.
If you’re self‑employed and a client reimburses you:
The reimbursement is often business income.
The underlying expense is a business expense.
Net effect: income minus expense.
Non‑reimbursed expenses
These are the ones you may be able to deduct as business expenses (if you’re self‑employed or otherwise eligible).
Label them clearly as “business – not reimbursed” and tie them to a specific client or project.

Key idea: Separate “reimbursed” from “business – not reimbursed” so you don’t double count or miss deductions.

3. What records to keep for tax purposes

For a client or business trip, many people keep:

1) Travel and lodging
Airfare / train / bus:
E‑ticket or receipt showing date, route, amount, and who traveled.
Hotel / lodging:
Itemized bill showing dates, nightly rate, taxes, and any extras (parking, Wi‑Fi, meals charged to room).
2) Local transportation
Rideshare/taxi/public transit:
Receipts or app screenshots with date, amount, and route.
Rental car:
Rental agreement, final invoice, and mileage log if you’re tracking miles.
Personal car:
Mileage log: date, starting/ending odometer or miles driven, destination, and business purpose.
You generally choose between standard mileage rate or actual expenses; the log matters either way.
3) Meals and entertainment
Receipts showing:
Date
Amount
Location
Ideally, who you met with and business purpose (you can write this on the receipt or store it in notes).
U.S. rules on meal deductibility (50%, 100%, etc.) change over time; the key is to document clearly.
4) Trip overview / log

Keep a simple trip log:

Dates of travel
Destination(s)
Main business purpose (e.g., “Onsite meetings with Client B – project kickoff”)
Rough schedule (e.g., “Mon–Wed: client meetings; Thu: personal sightseeing”)
5) Proof of payment
Credit card/bank statements that match your receipts.
If you used points + cash, keep the booking confirmation showing the breakdown.

Retention: Many people keep tax-related records for at least 3–7 years, depending on their risk tolerance and professional guidance.

Key idea: If you can answer “what, when, why, and how paid” for each expense, you’re in good shape.

4. Common pitfalls when mixing personal and business travel

When a trip is part business, part personal, watch out for:

A. Not allocating costs between business and personal

Example: You fly to a city for 2 days of client meetings, then stay 3 extra days for vacation.

Airfare might be fully business if the primary purpose is business.
Hotel may need to be split: 2 nights business, 3 nights personal.

Keep a clear record of which days were business vs personal.

B. Deducting personal side trips
Side trips (weekend in another city, tourist activities) are usually personal.
Track those separately so they don’t get mixed into business expenses.
C. Family or friends traveling with you

If a spouse/partner/friend comes along:

Their portion of airfare, meals, and lodging is usually personal, unless they’re legitimately working in the business.
If a hotel room costs the same whether they’re there or not, some people treat the full room cost as business for the business nights, but document why.
D. Double‑counting reimbursed expenses
If your employer or client reimburses you for an expense, you generally don’t also deduct it personally.
Good labeling (“reimbursed vs not reimbursed”) prevents this.
E. Poor documentation of business purpose
“Trip to NYC” is vague.
“Trip to NYC – meetings with Client X to review Q3 marketing plan” is much clearer.
Write the business purpose on receipts or in a log while it’s fresh.

Key idea: Be clear on what’s business vs personal, and write it down while you remember.
